title: Google I/O 2025 – rxKYK3DIzN0
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] PETER FRIESE: Firebase
is a powerful platform for building amazing apps. You've got robust databases,
authentication, hosting, and now AI
integration tools like the Firebase AI SDKs,
Genkit, and even vector embeddings
in Cloud Firestore. But let's be real. How do you use them? When does each make sense? And seriously, they
seem so similar. Do you need complex
backend work or can you keep it simple on the client
with the tools you already love? How do you make sure these AI
features are robust, secure, and production-ready, giving
your users a smooth experience? Hey, I'm Peter. And today, I'm going to
demystify all of this, showing you exactly how it
works and when to choose which Firebase AI tool. To show you how
these features can be used in a real-world
scenario, my team and I have been working
on a meal-planning app to collect and store
our favorite recipes. Users can add their own recipes,
browse them, and then get this beautifully
designed screen that helps them with the cooking. This is great, but we
found that it's often difficult to decide
what to cook. So we came up with
a couple of ideas to make it easier and
more fun for users to discover new recipes and help
them decide what to cook today. For example, a user might
want to surprise guests at a dinner party with
a meal in the style of a particular cuisine. We also wanted to
build something that helps the user
decide what to cook based on what's in their fridge. Just snap a picture of
what's in your fridge and get a suggestion
for a recipe. How amazing would that be? And of course, since users can
store their favorite recipes in this app, for example,
their grandma's apple crumble, we want to ensure the app
provides recipe suggestions that consider their
personal collection. To kick things off, we started
by prototyping a prompt in AI Studio. And honestly, the results
were pretty impressive. Check this out. We asked for a vegan
Turkish recipe for fall, and it gave us a full ingredient
list and detailed steps.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – rxKYK3DIzN0
text:
To kick things off, we started
by prototyping a prompt in AI Studio. And honestly, the results
were pretty impressive. Check this out. We asked for a vegan
Turkish recipe for fall, and it gave us a full ingredient
list and detailed steps. AI Studio has a
handy feature that gives you the working code in
the language of your choice for generating the
response from this prompt, including the code to
generate the recipe as JSON-structured output
so that we can easily parse and render the
result into the app's UI. To use this code
in our app, we need to make sure the app is
connected to Firebase and that it has the
Firebase SDK installed. Since our meal-planning app
uses Firebase to store recipes in Cloud Firestore, we've
already connected the app to a Firebase project. But if you'd like to learn
how to set up your app to use Firebase, including
creating a Firebase project and connecting your
app to Firebase, here is a video that walks
you through the steps. And since this is an iOS
app, adding the Firebase SDK to your app
is, of course, done using Swift Package Manager. With that out of the way, we
can connect our UI to the code. Now we can run the app
and try out a new feature. Let's see. I think I need a
dessert for four people. Ah, this sounds great. But wait, doesn't the
Gemini API require us to provide a Gemini API key? And isn't adding that API
key into our app's code base a security risk? It is indeed, as anyone
can now download the app, open it in a hex editor, and
exfiltrate your Gemini API key. A malicious actor can
then use your API key to make calls with it, racking
up charges on your account. So you should never, ever
put your Gemini API key directly into the
code base of any app that you want to make
available publicly. The best way to protect
your precious Gemini API key from being stolen
is to never put it into your app in
the first place. The good news is that
Firebase has built a solution to help secure your app
from these malicious actors.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – rxKYK3DIzN0
text:
The best way to protect
your precious Gemini API key from being stolen
is to never put it into your app in
the first place. The good news is that
Firebase has built a solution to help secure your app
from these malicious actors. When you use the Firebase AI
SDK to access generative models like Gemini, you don't put
a Gemini API key directly in your app. Instead, the key
stays on the server and can only be accessed
by a legitimate request from your app. This is achieved by using
Firebase App Check, which helps you to protect your
app's backend resources and make sure it only
serves requests that come in from your legitimate app
running on untampered devices. Now that we've got a basic
AI feature built in our app, we are ready to implement the
next, even cooler feature-- suggesting a recipe based on
a photo rather than only text. If you're anything
like me, you've stared in your fridge totally
stuck for dinner ideas. I usually fall back on
the same old recipes. But imagine if your
app could actually suggest something
new and exciting based on what's
already in your fridge. Users could simply
snap a photo, and we can combine that
snapshot with a text prompt describing their desired
dish, sending it to the AI. This combination of
visual data and text is called multimodal input. In this code
snippet, you can see that parsing the user's
photo to the model requires just a small
change to our code. We add the photo as an
additional parameter to the generateContent method. And that's it. Great. Let's give this a try. OK, so let me take a picture
and see what it comes up with. So I'm taking a picture of
this plate of fruit here. And yeah, this sounds delicious. But to be honest,
I am struggling to picture what this dish
actually should look like. Wouldn't it be
amazing if we could ask the model to generate an
image of the finished recipe? Using the Firebase AI
SDK, I can make a call to imagine our image
generation model and prompt it to generate
an inspirational picture of the dish based on
the text of the recipe.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – rxKYK3DIzN0
text:
ask the model to generate an
image of the finished recipe? Using the Firebase AI
SDK, I can make a call to imagine our image
generation model and prompt it to generate
an inspirational picture of the dish based on
the text of the recipe. Let's give this a try. And this is a lot more
inspiring than just looking at the text of the recipe. It's true. A picture is worth
a thousand words. So we've added a couple of
really cool features to our app with just a few lines of code. And we were able
to do all of this on the client using the
tools and programming language we know and love. However, you might have
noticed that all of the recipes that Gemini
suggested for us were based on its foundational
training data. And while I appreciate
that Gemini can surprise me with a wide variety
of recipes, our users might actually
prefer custom recipes that are based on the
collection of personal recipes they've stored in the app. For example, our users
might have a collection of recipes that have been
passed down several generations in the family. Or in a future
version of the app, we may decide to offer recipes
created by famous chefs as an in-app purchase. How can you implement this? Well, first, the
user takes a photo. Our app then uploads
that photo to Gemini and prompts it to
detect the ingredients. We should probably make sure
to get this list of ingredients as structured output to
make it easier to work with. Then we'll take this
list of ingredients and retrieve all of the
user's personal recipes that can be prepared
using those ingredients. And finally, we will
prompt the model to generate a new dish that
is inspired by these existing recipes. As you can see, we need quite
a few model capabilities to implement these features-- multimodal input to
understand what's in the photo of the
user's fridge, retrieval augmented generation to
fetch matching recipes from the database,
multimodal output to generate a new recipe with
corresponding image of the dish, tool used to call an API to
order any missing ingredients.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – rxKYK3DIzN0
text:
augmented generation to
fetch matching recipes from the database,
multimodal output to generate a new recipe with
corresponding image of the dish, tool used to call an API to
order any missing ingredients. Let's think about the
implementation of this feature. Sure, we could run
the entire process, detecting ingredients,
retrieving recipes, generating a new recipe, and even ordering
any missing ingredients directly in the app using
these client SDKs. But let's be honest. In this scenario, this might
not be the ideal solution, as you'd have to coordinate
different services and transfer potentially a
lot of data back and forth between your client
app and those services. By moving this complex
flow to the backend, we drastically reduce the amount
of data being transferred. This translates to faster
response times, a better user experience, and less strain
on the user's device, especially on spotty networks. And this is where
Genkit comes in. Genkit is Google's open-source
AI integration framework. It allows you to implement
AI flows using TypeScript. And we've just announced
support for Go and Python. Genkit flows are like
regular functions, which means you can do
anything that you would also be able to do in
any other function, like calling other APIs,
retrieving data from a database, performing
computations, et cetera. But the cool thing is that
Genkit flows are observable. And that means you can monitor
them using the AI Monitoring dashboard in the
Firebase console or any other tool that supports
OpenTelemetry, such as Google Cloud's Observability Suite. Genkit also provides
easy-to-use abstractions for working with models
and vector stores. And this enables
us to tap into any of the recipes our users have
stored in their app's database and use them to generate new
recipes based on the ingredients the user has on hand. Another really cool
feature of Genkit is its unified model interface. This allows you to
use different models by just providing the
name of the model you want to use instead.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – rxKYK3DIzN0
text:
and use them to generate new
recipes based on the ingredients the user has on hand. Another really cool
feature of Genkit is its unified model interface. This allows you to
use different models by just providing the
name of the model you want to use instead. This makes it super
easy to experiment with different models. Let's take a look at how
you would implement the meal planning use case with Genkit. First, we create a Genkit flow. Then we pass the user's
input to the model, prompting it to detect
which ingredients can be seen in the picture. By providing an
output schema, we can tell Genkit to
work with the model to return structured output,
which will make it easier for us to further process the result. Now that we have the
list of ingredients, we need to find
matching recipes that also fit the user's needs,
things dietary restrictions and preferred cuisines. That's no easy task when dealing
with complex search queries. Retrieving relevant
data from a database based on free-form inputs,
such as natural language or a list of ingredients,
is a common challenge in AI applications. That's where Genkit's retriever
abstractions comes in. It streamlines this
complex process, making it surprisingly
efficient. You can think of the retriever
as your intelligent search assistant. It takes the user's
request, translates it into a vector
embedding, and then uses nearest-neighbor search to
quickly locate the best matching recipes in your database. Vector embeddings
are essentially a way to turn text into
a series of numbers that a computer can understand. Here is the number
representation for apples. And here is the number
representation for oranges. Since these are vectors, we
can use mathematical functions like cosine to measure
how similar they are. This allows our
system to understand that apples and oranges are
both fruits, even though they are different. So it turns out you can
compare apples and oranges. Our recipes are stored
in Cloud Firestore, which supports vector embeddings. So when the user
stores a new recipe,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – rxKYK3DIzN0
text:
that apples and oranges are
both fruits, even though they are different. So it turns out you can
compare apples and oranges. Our recipes are stored
in Cloud Firestore, which supports vector embeddings. So when the user
stores a new recipe, we also calculate vector
embeddings for its ingredients and store them alongside it. Genkit's Firestore
retriever can then use those embeddings to quickly
find the recipes that best match a user's ingredients, like those
detected in their fridge photo. The result is a number
of Firestore documents that contain the best
matching recipes. Using these recipes,
we prompt the model to generate a brand-new
recipe for the specified number of people,
taking inspiration from the recipes
found in the database. This flow does a lot with
a few lines of code thanks to Genkit's powerful
AI abstractions. Now, you might be wondering
how we can include this Genkit flow in our mobile application. After all, our client
app is written in Swift, and the Genkit flow is
written in TypeScript. To bring those two
worlds together, we will first expose
the Genkit flow as a callable cloud function. This will allow us to deploy it
to Cloud Functions for Firebase or Cloud Run. We can then use the Firebase
client SDK to call this endpoint from our Swift app. So we've implemented two
exciting new features for our app, and we are ready
to ship them to production. But before we can
do that, we need to make sure that our app is
safe from unauthorized access. App Check is Firebase's
solution for keeping your app's backend resources safe. This works both for
Firebase resources as well as your
own custom backend. We can use App Check to
protect the backend services our app uses. First, we need to
register our app with App Check in the Firebase console. Next, we will add the App Check
library to our client app. Then we configure the App
Check provider we want to use. This needs to happen before you
initialize the Firebase SDK. Once you start out
rolling the app,

title: Google I/O 2025 – rxKYK3DIzN0
text:
Next, we will add the App Check
library to our client app. Then we configure the App
Check provider we want to use. This needs to happen before you
initialize the Firebase SDK. Once you start out
rolling the app, you will see your app's
requests showing up in the App Check section of the
Firebase console. You can now enforce
App Check for your app. And as soon as you do that,
only legitimate requests from your app will be permitted,
and all other requests will be blocked. Now that we implemented
these AI features in our app, we want to see how
they are performing both in test and production. To enable telemetry for your
Genkit flows and other actions, add the following lines to
your source, and then redeploy. The next time you call
your deployed Genkit flows, telemetry data
will be collected. To see a high-level
overview of the Genkit flows deployed in your
Firebase project, visit the Firebase console. At the top, you will see the
vitals of your Genkit flows, such as the number of
requests, the success rate, and the latency. At the bottom of
the screen, you can see a list of all the deployed
Genkit flows of your project. You can drill into
individual flows to see more details, most
importantly, how many tokens or images they
consumed or produced. But there is more. You can see the input
and output parameters. And when you click
on an invocation, you can see the entire
trace of your flow. With the trace
inspector, you can examine every step of the
flow invocation in detail. It reveals the input
and output of each step along with any
associated attributes. This allows for a granular
understanding of the flow's execution process. This is a really valuable
tool to help you gain insight into the performance of your
AI features and production. And it's a great help when
optimizing or debugging your Genkit flows. You can also inspect any
invocations that fail, like this one. This is a great help
for understanding why some of your invocations
might have failed. All of this is possible
because Genkit flows

title: Google I/O 2025 – rxKYK3DIzN0
text:
your Genkit flows. You can also inspect any
invocations that fail, like this one. This is a great help
for understanding why some of your invocations
might have failed. All of this is possible
because Genkit flows and all of their underlying
actions are observable. You don't have to add any
additional instrumentation. It's all built in. And there you have it. We've implemented a bunch
of exciting new features for our app, making
use of the AI services available from Firebase. We use the Firebase AI SDK to
call Gemini to generate recipes based on the user's input. We used both text input
and multimodal input, allowing our users to
generate recipes based on what's in their fridge. We then used multimodal
output to generate recipes, including inspirational
images showing what the meal should look like. We were able to implement all of
this in our client application using the same
tools and languages that we're used to
as mobile developers. We then switched it
up a gear and used Genkit to implement
a feature that grounds the model in the user's
data, which allows the app to provide results
that are based on the user's personal recipes. And finally, we made our
application ready for production and looked at how Firebase's
AI monitoring features can help us operate the AI features
of our app with confidence. So now you might be wondering
when you should use the Firebase AI SDKs versus Genkit. The answer is, it
depends on your use case. If you're implementing
features that don't require you to tap
into a large data set or data of any other backend
services, the Firebase AI SDKs are a great way to add powerful
AI features to your app. As you saw, it's
easy to get started, and you have access to a wide
spectrum of generative AI features. On the other hand, if
your use case requires you to tap into
a large data set, for example, a product
database, or if you need to connect your model
to other backend services, Genkit is the best choice. It allows you to
implement AI flows that integrate all those
various services
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – rxKYK3DIzN0
text:
you to tap into
a large data set, for example, a product
database, or if you need to connect your model
to other backend services, Genkit is the best choice. It allows you to
implement AI flows that integrate all those
various services and make them accessible to your
client application via callable API endpoints. Genkit is also the
go-to way if you'd like to integrate non-Google
models, like those from Anthropic or OpenAI. Finally, keep in mind that this
isn't an either/or decision. As you saw in our
example, you can have the best of both worlds
by using the Firebase AI SDKs and Genkit in the same app. Pick the best tool for the job. Thanks for watching, and I
will see you in the next one. [MUSIC PLAYING]
